What This Work Is: Non-discretionary, recurring work to operate, secure, and support existing services. Failure creates immediate operational, security, or compliance risk.
How to Decide (Tag Here If...): Required to meet SLAs, legal, security, or risk obligations. Keeps existing services running safely and reliably. Does not introduce new business capability.
Representative Examples: Emergency break-fix; production outages; routine monitoring, backups, DR exercises; control execution; security patching.

What This Work Is: Run work driven by the business to keep existing capabilities accurate, efficient, and effective without introducing new functionality.
How to Decide (Tag Here If...): Maintains or optimizes existing business rules or processes. Driven by accuracy, usability, or efficiency. No new products or material outcome changes.
Representative Examples: Configuration updates; process support changes; data quality fixes; loss-mitigation rule tweaks; model performance monitoring.

What This Work Is: Engineering work to keep the technology stack stable, secure, and modern without changing business capability.
How to Decide (Tag Here If...): Driven by tech debt, dependency hygiene, refactoring, decommissioning, or platform upgrades. No new business feature ships.
Representative Examples: Library upgrades; decommissioning legacy services; removing obsolete repos; YBYO / dev-environment cleanup; refactoring without behavior change.

What This Work Is: Run-the-business work mandated by regulation — controls, evidence, reporting that must continue to operate.
How to Decide (Tag Here If...): Driven by an ongoing regulatory obligation (not a new rule). Failure exposes regulatory or audit risk. Operational in nature.
Representative Examples: Quarterly stress-test execution; ongoing FFIEC reporting; existing OFAC screening operation; audit evidence gathering.

What This Work Is: Change-the-business work to comply with a new or amended regulation by a specified effective date.
How to Decide (Tag Here If...): Driven by a specific regulatory mandate with an effective date. Failure to deliver triggers non-compliance. Introduces new capability or modifies existing controls.
Representative Examples: CFPB rule changes; Reg X servicing-rule updates; new FHFA reporting requirement; AML / BSA enhancement program.

What This Work Is: Top enterprise-level investment chosen by exec leadership for outsized impact on growth, efficiency, or risk posture.
How to Decide (Tag Here If...): Named in the enterprise plan-of-record. Sponsored by an exec. Multi-quarter, cross-LoB. Tied to a measurable enterprise OKR.
Representative Examples: Same-day lender commitments program; loan-officer AI assist; enterprise data-platform migration; multi-year cloud modernization.

What This Work Is: Divisional-leadership priority — material impact on a single LoB (SF / MF / CapMkts) but not enterprise-wide.
How to Decide (Tag Here If...): Sponsored by a divisional SVP. Scoped within one LoB. Funded from divisional budget.
Representative Examples: SF underwriting workbench redesign; MF watchlist automation; CapMkts pricing-API consolidation.

What This Work Is: Smaller change-the-business work owned by a single block or product area — not divisional or enterprise priority.
How to Decide (Tag Here If...): Block-level scope. Block-level funding. Quarterly OKR. Not on the divisional or enterprise plan-of-record.
Representative Examples: Team-level dashboard improvements; small-scope feature flags; targeted UX tweaks; quarterly capability gap-fills.
